1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). But for
96	\emph{line bundles}, which are flat, that compatibility is \emph{elementary
97	flat-exactness}, not internal-hom machinery. This is exactly the carrier on
98	which the relative Picard group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
99	rests, and it is the subject of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,%
100	sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
101	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
102	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
103	
104	\section{Mathlib API survey}
105	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
106	
107	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
108	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
109	
110	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
111	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
112	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
113	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
114	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
115	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
116	sectionwise tensor product
117	\[
118	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
119	\]
120	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
121	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
122	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
123	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
124	
125	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
127	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
128	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
129	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
130	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
131	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
132	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
133	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
134	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
135	tensor-product operation
136	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
137	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
138	
139	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
140	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
141	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
142	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
143	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
144	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
145	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
146	\emph{flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
147	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
148	for line bundles \(L, M\) and the restriction along an open immersion
149	\(f\), the canonical comparison
150	\((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes M|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
151	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
152	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume, and for line bundles it is
153	elementary flat-exactness already available in Mathlib (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
154	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
155	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
156	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
157	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
158	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
159	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
160	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
161	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
162	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
163	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
164	independently of any upstream timing.
165	
166	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
167	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
173	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
175	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
176	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
177	  \[
178	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
179	  \]
180	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
181	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
182	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
183	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
184	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
185	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
186	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
187	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
188	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
189	  canonical compatibility
190	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
191	     \;\cong\;
192	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
193	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
194	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
195	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
196	
197	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
198	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
199	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
200	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
201	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
202	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
203	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
204	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
205	\end{definition}
206	
207	\begin{lemma}
208	
209	\leanok
210	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
211	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
213	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
214	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
215	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
216	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
217	  \[
218	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
219	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
220	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
221	  \]
222	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
223	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
224	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
225	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
226	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
227	  isomorphisms
228	  \[
229	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
230	    \qquad
231	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
232	  \]
233	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
234	  \[
235	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
236	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
237	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
238	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
239	  \]
240	  and a canonical braiding
241	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
242	  inherited from the corresponding data on
243	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
244	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
245	\end{lemma}
246	
247	\begin{proof}
248	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
249	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
250	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
251	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
252	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
253	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
254	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
255	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
256	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
257	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
258	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
259	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
260	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
261	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
262	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
263	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	\begin{remark}
267	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
268	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
269	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
270	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
271	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
272	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
273	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
274	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
275	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
276	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
277	  depend on it.
278	
279	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
280	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
281	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
282	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
283	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
284	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
285	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
286	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
287	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
288	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
289	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
290	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
291	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
292	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
293	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
294	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
295	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
296	
297	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
298	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the flat-scoped
299	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
300	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
301	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
302	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
303	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
304	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
305	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
306	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
307	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
308	\end{remark}
309	
310	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
311	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
312	
313	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
314	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
315	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
316	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
317	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
318	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility
319	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
320	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
321	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
322	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
323	cover, together with their restriction to the
324	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
325	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
326	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
327	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	[Tensor product commutes with restriction for line bundles]
331	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
332	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
333	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be line
334	  bundles (equivalently: locally-trivial rank-one modules, hence flat), and
335	  let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the restriction along an open immersion.
336	  The canonical comparison morphism
337	  \[
338	    (L \otimes_X M)\big|_f
339	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
340	    L\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; M\big|_f
341	  \]
342	  is an isomorphism. The intended Lean target is
343	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
348	  The statement is local on \(X\), so it suffices to verify it on affine
349	  opens, where both line bundles are presented by \(A\)-modules \(L, M\) over
350	  a commutative ring \(A\) and the comparison is a map of \(A\)-modules whose
351	  formation commutes with \(L \otimes_A (-)\). A line bundle is locally free
352	  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
353	  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
354	  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\)
355	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}, the
356	  \(\mathtt{instProjectiveOfInvertible}\) instance chain). For a flat module
357	  the comparison is then an isomorphism by ordinary exactness, with no
358	  internal-hom machinery: \(\otimes\) is always right-exact, so
359	  \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity, and flatness of \(L\) makes
360	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve injectivity
361	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
362	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
363	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
364	  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
365	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
366	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}): for invertible \(L\), the map
367	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) is bijective iff \(g\) is. Applying
368	  this to the (bijective) structural comparison map for the second factor
369	  \(M\) under restriction yields bijectivity of the full comparison, i.e.\
370	  the asserted isomorphism. The affine isomorphisms are compatible along an
371	  affine cover, so they glue to the global isomorphism.
372	
373	  This is the elementary flat-exactness replacement for the
374	  monoidal-closed / \(\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\,W\) route recorded in
375	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}: that route was forced only
376	  because the analogous \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability fails for a
377	  \emph{non-flat} module \(F\). Restricting the scope to line bundles makes
378	  the flatness hypothesis automatic, so the comparison is an isomorphism by
379	  module-theoretic exactness alone.
380	\end{proof}
381	
382	\begin{lemma}
383	
384	\leanok
385	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
386	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
388	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
389	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
390	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
391	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
392	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
393	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
394	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
395	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
396	\end{lemma}
397	
398	\begin{proof}
399	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
400	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
401	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
402	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
403	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
404	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
405	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
406	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
407	\end{proof}
408	
409	\begin{lemma}
410	[Associativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
411	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
412	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
413	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
414	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
415	  bundles. Then there exists an isomorphism
416	  \[
417	    (L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N
418	      \;\cong\;
419	    L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)
420	  \]
421	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently the proposition
422	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N \cong
423	  L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)\bigr)\) holds.
424	\end{lemma}
425	
426	\begin{proof}
427	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
428	  Choose a common affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\)
429	  trivialising \(L, M, N\) simultaneously. By
430	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} every tensor product
431	  occurring on either side is again a line bundle, hence flat, so
432	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} applies and both sides restrict to the
433	  iterated tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules on \(U_i\). There the associator
434	  \((L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i) \otimes_{A_i} N_i \cong
435	  L_i \otimes_{A_i} (M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i)\) is the standard associativity
436	  isomorphism of the tensor product of modules over a commutative ring. These
437	  local isomorphisms agree on overlaps --- naturality of the module
438	  associator against the restriction-comparison isomorphisms of
439	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and therefore glue to a global
440	  isomorphism. Only the \emph{existence} of the global isomorphism is
441	  asserted; no compatibility with a pentagon identity is claimed or needed.
442	\end{proof}
443	
444	\begin{lemma}
445	[Unit law for $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
446	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
447	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
448	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) a line
449	  bundle. Then \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\) and
450	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong L\); equivalently
451	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L)\) holds (and
452	  symmetrically on the right).
453	\end{lemma}
454	
455	\begin{proof}
456	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
457	  On a trivialising affine cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(L\), restriction
458	  commutes with \(\otimes_X\) by \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and the
459	  structure sheaf restricts to \(A_i\) as an \(A_i\)-module; the unitor
460	  \(A_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i \cong L_i\) is the standard left-unit isomorphism of
461	  the tensor product. These local isomorphisms glue to a global isomorphism
462	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\); the right-unit case is identical.
463	\end{proof}
464	
465	\begin{lemma}
466	[Commutativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
467	  \label{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
468	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
469	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
470	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
471	  bundles. Then \(L \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X L\); equivalently
472	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(L \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X L)\) holds.
473	\end{lemma}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
477	  On a common trivialising affine cover, restriction commutes with
478	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}); locally the braiding
479	  \(L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i \cong M_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i\) is the standard
480	  symmetry isomorphism of the tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules. The local
481	  symmetries glue to a global isomorphism.
482	\end{proof}
483	
484	\begin{lemma}
485	
486	\leanok
487	[Inverse of an invertible module]
488	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
490	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
491	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
492	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
493	  bundle. The dual
494	  \[
495	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
496	  \]
497	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
498	  isomorphism
499	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
500	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
501	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
502	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
503	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
504	\end{lemma}
505	
506	\begin{proof}
507	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
508	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
509	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
510	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
511	  The contraction map
512	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
513	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
514	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
515	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
516	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
517	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
518	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
519	\end{proof}
520	
521	\begin{lemma}
522	
523	\leanok
524	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
525	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
527	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
528	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
529	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
530	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
531	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
532	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
533	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
534	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
535	  \[
536	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
537	    \;\;=\;\;
538	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
539	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
540	  \]
541	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
542	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
543	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
544	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
545	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
546	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
547	\end{lemma}
548	
549	\begin{proof}
550	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
551	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
552	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
553	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
554	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
555	  Direct from
556	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
557	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
558	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
559	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
560	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
561	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
562	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
563	  associativity, unit and commutativity isomorphisms of
564	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
565	  are isomorphisms of line bundles, hence restrict to the subtype;
566	  together with the inverse of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
567	  they equip the isomorphism classes of
568	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with the full
569	  group-law data (associativity, unit, commutativity, inverse).
570	\end{proof}
571	
572	\begin{lemma}
573	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
574	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
575	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
576	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
577	  functor
578	  \[
579	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
580	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
581	  \]
582	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
583	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
584	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
585	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
586	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
587	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
588	  canonical isomorphism
589	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
590	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
591	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
592	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
593	\end{lemma}
594	
595	\begin{proof}
596	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
597	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
598	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
599	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
600	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
601	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
602	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
603	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the same
604	  flat-exactness / restriction-compatibility argument as in
605	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (extension of scalars along \(A \to B\)
606	  is itself flat, so the comparison is an isomorphism). The compatibility
607	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
608	  same cover.
609	\end{proof}
610	
611	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
612	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
613	
614	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
615	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
616	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
617	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
618	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
619	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
620	
621	\begin{theorem}
622	
623	\leanok
624	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
625	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
626	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
627	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
628	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
629	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
630	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
631	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
632	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
633	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
634	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
635	
636	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
637	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
638	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
639	  % groups)
640	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
641	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
642	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
643	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
644	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
645	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
646	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
647	  % \end{dfn}
648	  %  [\dots]
649	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
650	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
651	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
652	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
653	  % topologies  by
654	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
655	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
656	  %  \end{dfn}"
657	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
658	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
659	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
660	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
661	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
662	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
663	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
664	  \[
665	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
666	  \]
667	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
668	  abelian-group structure with addition
669	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
670	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
671	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
672	  inverse
673	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
674	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
675	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
676	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
677	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
678	  map
679	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
680	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
681	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
682	\end{theorem}
683	
684	\begin{proof}
685	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
686	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
687	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
688	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
689	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
690	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
691	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
692	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
693	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
694	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
695	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
696	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
697	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
698	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
699	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
700	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
701	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
702	  read on isomorphism classes:
703	  \begin{itemize}
704	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
705	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
706	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
707	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
708	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
709	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
710	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
711	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
712	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
713	  \end{itemize}
714	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
715	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
716	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
717	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
718	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
719	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
720	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
721	  transported from
722	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
723	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
724	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
725	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
726	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
727	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
728	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
729	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
730	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
731	  verbatim.
732	
733	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
734	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
735	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
736	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
737	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
738	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
739	
740	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
741	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
742	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
743	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
744	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
745	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
746	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
747	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
748	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
749	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
750	\end{proof}
751	
752	\section{LOC estimates and prover-lane sequencing}
753	\label{sec:tensorobj_loc_estimates}
754	
755	The substrate splits into three logical pieces; each is one prover lane.
756	The route is deliberately \emph{lighter} than a monoidal-category build:
757	no pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence is discharged, and no
758	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure is constructed (cf.\
759	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
760	
761	\paragraph{Piece 1: definition + functoriality + well-definedness on
762	iso-classes
763	(\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}).}
764	The Lean target file is the new
765	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. The body
766	consists of:
767	\begin{itemize}
768	  \item a definition
769	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} taking two
770	    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) objects and producing one, via
771	    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) on the underlying
772	    presheaves and the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site;
773	  \item the functorial action on morphisms (bifunctoriality, identity
774	    and composition laws), enough to transport an isomorphism
775	    \(L \cong L_0\) to \(L \otimes M \cong L_0 \otimes M\) --- i.e.\
776	    well-definedness of the operation on isomorphism classes.
777	\end{itemize}
778	Estimate: approximately 30--60 LOC project-side. (No full natural-iso
779	associator/unitor/braiding \emph{coherence} data is needed here; only the
780	bifunctorial action and the iso-transport.)
781	
782	\paragraph{Piece 2: the flat-scoped restriction iso + the four
783	existence-of-iso lemmas
784	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
785	       lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
786	       lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
787	       lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
788	       lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
789	       lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).}
790	The same file, building on Piece 1. The body consists of:
791	\begin{itemize}
792	  \item the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism
793	    \texttt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso} --- the single genuinely-hard
794	    ingredient --- via elementary flat-exactness: \(\otimes\) is
795	    right-exact and \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)
796	    gives injectivity-preservation, packaged for invertible modules as
797	    \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\) (both in
798	    Mathlib); the flatness is automatic since
799	    \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{Module.Projective}
800	    \Rightarrow \mathtt{Module.Flat}\);
801	  \item locally-trivial-of-rank-one is preserved by \(\otimes\) and by
802	    dual on a common trivialising cover;
803	  \item the four existence-of-iso lemmas (associativity, unit,
804	    commutativity, inverse), each a \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong
805	    \cdots)\) assembled from \texttt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso} on a common
806	    trivialising cover.
807	\end{itemize}
808	Estimate: approximately 30--60 LOC project-side, dominated by
809	\texttt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}; the four iso lemmas are short once it is
810	in place. This is materially lighter than a full
811	coherence-axiom discharge, because no pentagon/triangle/hexagon identity

36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97

100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope

109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>

115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")

119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```

If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.

44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.

1	# `tensorObj_restrict_iso` — precise missing ingredient (iter-206 Lane TS)
2	
3	## Target
4	
5	```lean
6	noncomputable def tensorObj_restrict_iso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
7	    [IsOpenImmersion f] (M N : X.Modules) :
8	    (tensorObj M N).restrict f ≅ tensorObj (M.restrict f) (N.restrict f)
9	```
10	where `tensorObj M N := (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
11	(PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj M.val N.val)`.
12	
13	## Progress this iter (in the file, compiles GREEN)
14	
15	The proof now performs **two real reduction steps** before the residual `sorry`:
16	
17	1. `Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback f` — reduces `· .restrict f` to
18	   `(pullback f).obj ·`. (Mathlib, `AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`.)
19	2. `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback f.toRingCatSheafHom` — **the key
20	   lemma surfaced this iter**: `sheafification ⋙ pullback φ ≅
21	   PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom ⋙ sheafification`
22	   (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackContinuous`). Moves the
23	   pullback *inside* the sheafification. This **discharges half (ii)** of the
24	   obstruction recorded in earlier iters ("sheafification commutes with
25	   pullback") — it IS in Mathlib, contrary to the old docstring claim.
26	
27	After these the goal is the **purely presheaf-level** residual
28	```
29	sheafify ((PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ N.val))
30	  ≅ (M.restrict f).tensorObj (N.restrict f)
31	```
32	
33	## The single precise missing ingredient
34	
35	A **`Monoidal` (or at least oplax-monoidal-with-iso-comparison) instance for
36	`PresheafOfModules.pullback φ`** w.r.t. `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`,
37	i.e. the base-change isomorphism
38	```
39	(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (A ⊗ B)
40	   ≅ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj A ⊗ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj B.
41	```
42	
43	### Why flatness does NOT shortcut this (correction to the iter-206 plan framing)
44	
45	The plan's "re-scope to line bundles ⇒ flat-exactness closes it" addresses only
46	*iso-ness* of a comparison map. But `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is defined as
47	an **abstract left adjoint** `(pushforward φ).leftAdjoint`
48	(`Mathlib/.../Presheaf/Pullback.lean:44`) — there is **no sectionwise formula**,
49	so there is no canonical comparison map to even state, let alone prove iso. The
50	comparison map is the **oplax-monoidal structure of the left adjoint of the
51	(strong) monoidal `pushforward`** (doctrinal/mate of `pushforward`'s lax
52	structure). That oplax structure is what is absent from Mathlib; flatness would
53	only upgrade it from a map to an iso *after* it exists. So re-scoping the
54	signature to line bundles is **not** sufficient to unblock — the blocker is
55	upstream of the flatness step.
56	
57	### What IS present in Mathlib (so the gap is "lift", not "invent")
58	
59	- `ModuleCat.extendScalars` **is** `Monoidal`
60	  (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction:42`; `μ`/`ε` via
61	  `AlgebraTensorModule` base-change isos). This is the *sectionwise* statement.
62	- `PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀OfCommRingCat` **is** `Monoidal`
63	  (`.../Presheaf/PushforwardZeroMonoidal.lean:33`) — but only because reindexing
64	  has `μIso = Iso.refl`; the extension-of-scalars lift is genuine work.
65	- `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`, `pullbackIso`,
66	  `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`, `hom_ext` (gluing) — all present.
67	
68	### Suggested route for a future `mathlib-build` lane
69	
70	Build, project-side, a `(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ).Monoidal` (or an explicit
71	`PresheafOfModules.pullbackMonoidalComparison` iso) by **lifting
72	`ModuleCat.extendScalars.Monoidal` sectionwise** through the
73	partial-left-adjoint construction, OR by the doctrinal-adjunction route
74	(left adjoint of strong-monoidal `pushforward` is oplax; strong because each
75	section is the `extendScalars` strong-monoidal comparison). Then
76	`tensorObj_restrict_iso` closes as
77	`sheafification.mapIso (pullbackMonoidalComparison …) ≪≫ (units bookkeeping)`,
78	where the units bookkeeping relates `sheafify (pullback M.val)` to
79	`M.restrict f` via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`/`pullbackIso`.
80	
81	This is the same *class* of multi-file Mathlib gap as the abandoned
82	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` route, but **strictly smaller**: the
83	sectionwise ingredient already exists, and the sheafification half is now
84	discharged by `sheafificationCompPullback`.
85	
86	## Downstream (all blocked on the above)
87	
88	`tensorObj_assoc_iso`, `tensorObj_comm_iso`, the arbitrary-`L` unit iso,
89	`exists_tensorObj_inverse`, and `addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` all consume
90	`tensorObj_restrict_iso` (global associator/unitor/braiding need it on a common
91	trivialising cover, glued via `isIso_iff_isIso_app`). They cannot close until
92	the base-change instance lands.
93

102	### Coverage

They are correctly blocked on `tensorObj_restrict_iso`; the prover deliberately withheld scaffold sorries for them. The blueprint does not currently signal this blocking relationship inline on those blocks.
106	
107	### Proof-sketch depth: **under-specified for `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` (MUST-FIX)**
108	
109	The proof sketch for `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` (blueprint lines 346–380) is the central adequacy failure. It describes a flat-exactness argument with three narrative steps:
110	
111	> "The statement is local on X, so it suffices to verify it on affine opens, where both line bundles are presented by A-modules L, M over a commutative ring A and **the comparison is a map of A-modules** whose formation commutes with L ⊗_A (-). ... For a flat module the comparison is then an isomorphism by ordinary exactness. ... The affine isomorphisms are compatible along an affine cover, so they glue to the global isomorphism."
112	
113	The claimed Mathlib citations are `Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap` and `Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff`.
114	
115	**Why this proof sketch is not formalizable as written:**
116	
117	The sketch asserts "the comparison is a map of A-modules" and then applies flatness to upgrade it to an iso. But in Mathlib at `b80f227`, `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is defined as an **abstract left adjoint** (`(pushforward φ).leftAdjoint`, `Mathlib/.../Presheaf/Pullback.lean:44`) with **no sectionwise formula**. Therefore:
118	
119	- There is **no comparison map** `(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ N.val) → (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj M.val ⊗ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj N.val` in Mathlib.
120	- The comparison map is the **oplax-monoidal structure** (the mate of the strong-monoidal `pushforward`) of this functor, and it has not been constructed in Mathlib.
121	- Flatness would only upgrade this map from a map to an iso **after** it exists; it does not construct it.
122	- **Re-scoping the signature to line bundles does not unblock this**: flatness is downstream of the map construction, not a bypass of it.
123	
124	The prover's two genuine reduction steps (`restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `sheafificationCompPullback`) already discharge the "sheafification commutes with pullback" part that was previously an obstacle, leaving the goal as exactly this comparison-map construction. The prover verified that `sheafificationCompPullback` IS in Mathlib (correcting the old docstring), but the comparison at the presheaf level is not.
125	
126	**What the blueprint proof sketch should say instead:**
127	The proof sketch should acknowledge that:
128	1. Step 1 (localization + gluing) is available.
129	2. Step 2 (sheafification commutes with pullback) is dischargeable via `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`.
130	3. Step 3 (the presheaf-level base-change iso `pullback(A ⊗ B) ≅ pullback A ⊗ pullback B`) requires a project-side construction: an oplax-monoidal (or Monoidal) instance for `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` lifting `ModuleCat.extendScalars.Monoidal` (which IS in Mathlib sectionwise at `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction:42`) through the partial-left-adjoint construction. This is a `mathlib-build` task, not a single-lane close.
131	4. Only after step 3 does flatness become relevant (to confirm the resulting map is an iso for line bundles — though in fact the same argument works for all flat modules, and `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is strong-monoidal for flat maps, so the comparison would be an iso without the line-bundle restriction).
132	
133	**Lean → blueprint assessment:** The Lean code is correctly advancing; the sorry placement and the precise residual documented in L260–281 and in `informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md` are accurate and well-diagnosed.
134	
135	**Blueprint → Lean assessment:** The proof sketch misleads a future prover into believing flat-exactness Mathlib lemmas close the goal directly, when those lemmas are downstream of a comparison-map construction that is absent from Mathlib. A prover following the sketch would not understand why `Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff` cannot be applied to the current Lean goal. **This is a must-fix blueprint adequacy failure.**
136

# Session 206 — review of iter-206

## Metadata

- **Single prover lane**: `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean` (Lane TS), mode `prove`, status `done`.
- **Sorry trajectory**: TS file **4 → 3** (net −1). Global ~81 → ~80. Build GREEN (`lake env lean` exit 0; only deprecation + 3 `sorry` warnings). **Zero `axiom` declarations** (blueprint-doctor confirms none project-wide).
- **COE**: not dispatched (escalation pause honored, now its 3rd consecutive iter).
- **Targets attempted**: `tensorObj_restrict_iso` (advanced, still residual sorry); `monoidalCategory` instance + 2 transport lemmas (removed — net −1); `exists_tensorObj_inverse` / `addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (untouched, blocked).

## The defining event: the iter-206 plan's pre-committed reversal signal FIRED

The iter-206 plan adopted the **TS flat/line-bundle pivot** (`## Decision made`) on the
premise that `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, re-scoped to line bundles, reduces to
"elementary flat-exactness already available in Mathlib," bypassing the
`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` wall. The plan explicitly pre-committed:
*"the TS prover finding that the comparison-map construction `(L⊗M)|_f → L|_f ⊗ M|_f`
cannot be built at the sheaf level without the monoidal machinery after all … re-evaluate
next iter."*

**That signal fired this iter.** The prover (and, independently, lean-vs-blueprint-checker
ts-iter206) found:

- `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is an **abstract left adjoint** (`(pushforward φ).leftAdjoint`,
  `Mathlib/.../Presheaf/Pullback.lean:44`) with **no sectionwise formula**, so the
  base-change comparison **map itself is absent** from Mathlib — it is the oplax-monoidal
  structure (mate of the strong-monoidal `pushforward`), not yet constructed.
- **Flatness does NOT shortcut this**: it only upgrades an already-existing map to an iso.
  Re-scoping the signature to line bundles is therefore **not** sufficient to unblock; the
  blocker is upstream of the flatness step.

So the flat-pivot's central premise is partially **disproven**. TS now sits on the **same
class of multi-file absent-Mathlib gap** as COE (which is paused): a monoidal/oplax structure
on a pullback/localization of presheaves of modules. The gap is a *lift* (the sectionwise
`ModuleCat.extendScalars.Monoidal` exists at `.../ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean:42`),
not an invention — but it is a `mathlib-build`-scale lane, not a single prove lane.

## Real progress this iter (genuine, but not a critical-path sorry closure)

1. **Removed the dead `monoidalCategory := sorry` instance** + the two off-path
   `MonoidalClosed`-route supplements (`isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`,
   `monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`) and the `MathlibSupplementMonoidalSheafification`
   section. This **clears the iter-203/204/205 contamination guard** (the sorry-bearing
   `instance` flowing `sorryAx` to consumers). Net −1 sorry. lean-auditor confirmed no
   dangling references to the removed decls.
2. **`tensorObj_restrict_iso` advanced** from a bare-sorry-with-comment to **two genuine
   Mathlib reduction steps** before its residual `sorry`:
   - Step 1: `Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback f` (restrict ≅ pullback).
   - Step 2: `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback f.toRingCatSheafHom` — **this IS in
     Mathlib**, correcting the prior multi-iter docstring claim that
     "sheafification commutes with pullback" was absent. Discharges half the obstruction.
   - Residual goal: `sheafify ((PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ N.val)) ≅
     (M.restrict f).tensorObj (N.restrict f)` — the missing presheaf base-change iso.
   Full write-up: `informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md`.

This is the same *progress shape* that defined COE's stall: each iter lands "the foundational
input" (a precisely-pinned residual) while the visible critical-path sorry count does not
move. Net −1 this iter is a dead-declaration removal, not a critical-path closure.

## Dead-ends recorded (do NOT retry — see recommendations.md)

- Adding `IsLocallyTrivial`/`Module.Invertible` hyps to close via flatness: does not reach
  the comparison-map construction (upstream of flatness).
- Relating `(M.restrict f).val` to `(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj M.val` at the pure
  presheaf level: adjoint on opposite sides, iso only after sheafification — circular without
  the base-change instance.

## Subagent findings (full reports linked; do not re-read raw here)

- **lean-vs-blueprint-checker ts-iter206** (`task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-ts-iter206.md`):
  1 **must-fix (F1)** — the blueprint `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` proof sketch is **not
  formalizable as written** (claims flat-exactness closes the goal; omits the comparison-map
  construction that is the real blocker). 4 **major** (M1 missing `\lean{}` pin on
  `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` — *fixed this review*; M2 `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`
  overpromises λ/ρ/α/β with no Lean decls; M3 `lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct` scope mismatch;

re-evaluate if the prover finds the comparison map cannot be built at the sheaf level without
the monoidal machinery. **The prover found exactly that** (confirmed independently by
lean-vs-blueprint-checker ts-iter206, must-fix F1): `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is an
abstract left adjoint with no sectionwise formula, so the base-change comparison **map itself
is absent** from Mathlib; **flatness only upgrades an existing map to an iso, it does not
construct one**, so re-scoping to line bundles does NOT bypass the gap.

**TS is now in the same regime as COE**: blocked on a multi-file absent-Mathlib monoidal/oplax
structure (here, a `Monoidal`/oplax-comparison instance for `PresheafOfModules.pullback`,
lifting the sectionwise `ModuleCat.extendScalars.Monoidal` through the partial-left-adjoint
construction; precise recipe in `informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md`).

**Fork for the planner (pick one; do not assign a 5th "foundational input" helper round):**
- **(a) Open a dedicated `mathlib-build` lane** to construct
  `PresheafOfModules.pullbackMonoidalComparison` (or `.Monoidal`). Pro: precise recipe in hand,
  sectionwise ingredient exists. Con: multi-file, Mathlib-PR-scale — the same cost class that
  paused COE; consult mathlib-analogist (cross-domain-inspiration) on the doctrinal-adjunction
  mate route before committing.
- **(b) Pause TS** as COE was paused (escalation-style), pending USER direction.

  — the iter-206 plan already raised the **Albanese-UP / RR-free A.2.c excision** as the TOP
  open question (iter-207 strategy-auditor committed). This is the productive escape: it can
  excise the project's single most-stuck node AND sidesteps the monoidal-on-pullback wall.

Recommendation: **(c) as the primary iter-207 move** (run the committed strategy-auditor on the
Albanese route first), with **(b)** for TS in the interim. Reserve **(a)** only if the USER
directs a Mathlib-infrastructure build.

## 2. (must-fix, blueprint) Rewrite the `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` proof sketch — F1

The chapter (rewritten iter-206) claims the proof is "elementary flat-exactness already
available in Mathlib." This is **not formalizable as written** and would mislead a future prover
into applying `Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff` to a goal where no comparison map exists.

the four deferred blocks). NOTE: the review already added the `\lean{}` pin (M1) and a `% NOTE:`
flagging F1 — the writer should fold the NOTE's content into a corrected proof body.

3rd consecutive iter with no COE dispatch. Blocker unchanged (Stacks 02JK conormal-localisation
iso / Stacks 00OE–00TT). USER decision territory. The iter-206 plan reframed the 02JK/Thm-3.2
cone as **EXCISION-PENDING** behind the Albanese-UP route — resolving #1(c) likely also resolves
COE's status. Do not re-open COE as a prove lane without a USER hint or a concrete 02JK-free path.

- `RelPicFunctor.lean` (HELD): `PicSharp := const PUnit` (L330), `functorial := 0` (L377) —

## 5. Reusable patterns discovered this iter

- **`SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback` IS in Mathlib** (`.../Sheaf/PullbackContinuous.lean`):
  `sheafification ⋙ pullback φ ≅ PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom ⋙ sheafification`. Corrects a

(2) **`isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`** — `MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal W` decomposes to the SINGLE hypothesis `whiskerLeft : W g → W (F ◁ g)`: `IsMultiplicative W` is a free instance (inverseImage of a localizing property), and `whiskerRight` follows from `whiskerLeft` via the symmetric braiding: `(W.arrow_mk_iso_iff (Arrow.isoMk (β_ F₁ G) (β_ F₂ G))).2 (hwl hf G)` (requires the source category braided/symmetric, which `PresheafOfModules R₀` is). **Reusable** for any "monoidal structure descends through a localization" need; the two `def`s are permanent infrastructure that the eventual `whiskerLeft` discharge plugs straight into. **Syntactic-vs-defeq caveat for the scheme-level wiring**: the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal instance keys on `X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat` while the sheafification instances auto-resolve only for `X.ringCatSheaf.val` — `rfl`-defeq but synthesised syntactically, so the two forms must be bridged by hand with `inferInstanceAs`.

The instance injects `sorryAx` transitively into every downstream consumer; the def localizes the sorry. **Live example**: `TensorObjSubstrate.monoidalCategory` is still `noncomputable instance … := sorry` (L150) — flagged by the iter-203 lean-auditor, NOT yet fixed as of iter-204 (its sorry tensorUnit is exactly why `exists_tensorObj_inverse`'s `𝟙_` is opaque). iter-205 should either land the real proof (a `MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` instance for the module-sheafification localization — see the TS notes) or demote the instance to a `def` to contain the sorry.

Re-verify a long-standing blocker before budgeting more iters around it.

Three reusable substrate pieces: (1) **`quotSMulTop_quotientRing_linearEquiv (r)` : `QuotSMulTop r A ≃ₗ[A⧸span{r}] A⧸span{r}`** — `QuotSMulTop r A` (= `A⧸(r•⊤)`) is NOT defeq to `A⧸span{r}`; build the equiv explicitly from `QuotSMulTop.equivQuotTensor` + `TensorProduct.rid` + `LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfSurjective` along surjective `mk`, transport `IsRegular` with `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr`. The head's NZD-ness comes from `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.isDomain_of_regularLocal` + `IsSMulRegular.of_ne_zero`; the quotient stays regular local via `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq`. **The landed signature drops the blueprint's `(hn : ringKrullDim A = n)` hypothesis** — the dimension is read off `spanFinrank` internally. **Reusable** for any "linearly-independent cotangent classes descend / form a regular sequence" need. It is not. Two facts: (i) `Scheme.Modules X = SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf`, and `X.ringCatSheaf.val` is *definitionally* `X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat` (`Scheme.ringCatSheaf` is `@[reducible]`), so for `M : X.Modules`, `M.val` is exactly what `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf)` consumes — no change-of-rings reconciliation; (ii) `PresheafOfModules.sheafification (α : R₀ ⟶ R.val) [...] : PresheafOfModules R₀ ⥤ SheafOfModules R` exists and is axiom-clean (for `R := X.ringCatSheaf`, `α := 𝟙 …`, all four instances auto-resolve for the opens site). `tensorObj := (sheafification …).obj (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj M.val N.val)`; functoriality via `(sheafification …).map (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom f.val g.val)`. **Two reusable gotchas**: (a) **`AddCommGrpCat`, NOT `AddCommGrp`** — `HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology Y) AddCommGrpCat.{u}` synthesizes only under the current name; the deprecated alias `AddCommGrp` does NOT carry the instance (cost most of a session). Closure recipe for `_succ_pd`: restructure as `induction k generalizing M`. **Inductive step (`pd M = k+2`)**: minimal surjection `f : R^n ↠ M`; `projectiveDimension_ker_eq_of_surjection` squeezes `pd(ker f)=k+1` exactly (`projectiveDimension_le_iff` upper bound + `projectiveDimension_ge_iff` contrapositive lower bound, both off the iter-200 `HasProjectiveDimensionLT` ascent/descent helpers); IH on `ker f`; `Module.depth_ses_ineqs_of_surjection_finite_localRing` packages `depth_of_short_exact` parts (2)(3) (with `depth(R^n)=depth(R)` via `depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty`); `enat_ab_inductive_combine` does the `ℕ∞` arithmetic. **Base case (`pd M = 1`)**: consumes the iter-201 matrix-collapse substrate — `ker f` free, SES `0→R^k→[A]R^n→[f]M→0` with `A`'s entries in `𝔪=Ann κ`; `exists_ne_zero_ext_of_depth_eq` gives nonzero `α:Ext^D(κ,R^k)`, matrix-collapse kills `α.comp(mk₀(ofHom A))`, `covariant_sequence_exact₁` transports to `Ext^{D'}(κ,M)`, `Order.add_one_le_of_lt` finishes. **Stacks 00MF was OBVIATED** by this route (no "what is exact" criterion needed). **Key `ℕ∞`/Lean idioms (reusable)**: (a) `ℕ∞ = WithTop ℕ` has NO `IsStrictOrderedRing` — reduce `min`/`sub`/`+` facts to `ℕ` disjunctions via `Nat.cast_*` then `omega`; (b) use `by gcongr` (not `add_le_add_right`) for monotone `+1` on `ℕ∞`; (c) `ShortComplex.mk … (zero proof)` discharges via `rw [← ModuleCat.ofHom_comp, show f ∘ₗ A = 0 …]; rfl` (NOT `simp`); (d) free-kernel basis via `(Module.finBasis R K).equivFun.symm`, with `k≥1` proved by subsingleton contradiction; (e) after `induction k generalizing M` the IH takes only the pd hypothesis. **Namespace caveat**: the de-privatised cross-file helpers are `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.isDomain_of_regularLocal` and `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq` (NOT bare `RingTheory.…`).
242	
243	- **`stalkSpecializes`/`germ` naturality: term-mode over `rw`, and `h_compat` discharge by defeq** *(NEW iter-202 Archon canonical — Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 3 in `RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:572-621`, 2 axiom-clean decls; HARD BAR.)* For morphism-level naturality of `stalkSpecializes` to a generic point (germ-chase via `TopCat.Presheaf.stalk_hom_ext`): (a) `(F.stalkCongr e).hom = F.stalkSpecializes e.ge` is `rfl` — there is NO Mathlib `stalkCongr_hom` lemma; introduce a local `have hcongr := by intros; rfl`.
